Geological hazards

All circles are area-proportional to affected people (√ scale); the smallest are floored to a minimum size for visibility. The three affected-people legends use independent scales. Sarangani earthquake shows persons affected by city/municipality (NDRRMC SitRep No. 21, 30 Jun 2026); historical events show Total Affected (EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain). The Organizations and 5W layers both size circles by number of organizations and share one common scale, so they can be compared directly; the 5W layer counts distinct organizations responding per city/municipality; use the disaster checkboxes to recompute it for selected responses (5W Consolidated Data, OCHA Philippines). 32 EM-DAT records lacking coordinates or affected figures are not shown. Storm tracks show Category 3–5 cyclones (2010–2024) coloured by peak Saffir–Simpson category (IBTrACS, NOAA NCEI); tracks are clipped to the Philippines area of interest, so each line covers only the 3–21 hours of the track inside that boundary rather than the full ocean path, and 8 storms retaining a single fix are drawn as dots. The El Niño layer shows the PAGASA dry-condition outlook to end-December 2026 (issued 22 Jun 2026; basis: Dec 2025–21 Jun 2026 observed and Jul–Dec 2026 forecast rainfall) by province/adm2, using PAGASA's own class colours; boundaries are simplified for file size, and City of Isabela and the Special Geographic Area were not enumerated by PAGASA (classification inherited from Basilan and Cotabato respectively). Active volcano markers show the 24 volcanoes listed as active by DOST–PHIVOLCS (Volcanoes of the Philippines, compiled 15 Jul 2026); markers are anchored at the summit coordinates and are drawn without collision suppression, so closely spaced pairs such as Babuyan Claro and Smith overlap at low zoom. Active fault lines are vector traces classified Active (fccode 01) from the DOST–PHIVOLCS ActiveFault ArcGIS service. This extract contains 2,000 features and is flagged by the service as truncated (exceededTransferLimit), so an unknown number of active faults are not shown; potentially active faults (fccode 02) and trenches are excluded by design. Strongest earthquakes are the 30 magnitude 6.7+ events recorded in the Philippines, the Philippine Islands region or immediately offshore since 2000 (epicentre coordinates; magnitudes are preferred values and may differ slightly between USGS and PHIVOLCS); marker size scales with magnitude. These are epicentres of the largest events and are distinct from the Disaster events layer, which shows EM-DAT earthquakes sized by people affected. Anticipatory action shows the 13 provinces listed as covered — Bicol (Albay, Camarines Sur, Camarines Norte, Catanduanes, Sorsogon), Eastern Visayas (Eastern Samar, Northern Samar, Samar, Leyte, Southern Leyte) and Caraga (Surigao del Norte, Surigao del Sur, Dinagat Islands). One icon marks each province at a point inside its largest landmass; it denotes province-wide coverage, not a specific site. Province boundaries follow COD-AB, which places the Surigao/Dinagat provinces in Region XIII (Caraga). Basemap © Mapbox © OpenStreetMap · OCHA ROAP.
